quotes.quote: Computers may change, but people don't.
The average programmer reads less than one technical book per year. The very act of reading this book already sets you apart from probably ninety percent of your fellow developers.

The vast majority of software development projects will fail: they will overrun their schedules, produce substandard results, or sometimes not even finish at all. This isn't an argument; it's a statistical fact.
Half of success is not repeating the same mistakes you, or other people, have made.
It doesn't matter how many 'coding superstars' you've got when none of them can talk to each other, or agree on anything.
Developers are freakish, extreme users at best—'Homo Logicus' versus 'Homo Sapiens.'
Designing software is difficult, to be sure, but designing a door is difficult too.
Remember, absolute power corrupts absolutely. But it also rocks absolutely.
Writing code all day sort of saps my will to come home and... write more code.
Life's just too short, and besides, never write what you can steal – right?
I suspect the .NET solutions may have been more advanced, but this is the one that was easiest to get running. Easy is good.
Also, I'm really lazy.
Note that Server 2003 has enhanced 'lockdown' security out of the box – meaning, it won't run jack squat without manual intervention.
Dogtags for your application, if you will.
If you're whipping out dogtags on a regular basis, that's symptomatic of a deeper problem.
The About Box is not meant to be used every day, but when you need it, it can be a lifesaver.
Decorative About Boxes are not helpful.
It's OK to be used infrequently, but it also needs to provide decent diagnostic info.
After working with VB6 and 'classic' ASP for so long, I got spoiled with effectively nonexistent compile times.
The Athlon 64 is absolutely mopping the floor with everything else.
It certainly can't be clock speed, since the Athlon 64 runs at ~ 2ghz.
The fastest CPU compiles debug releases almost twice as fast as the 'slowest' CPU.
Depending on how much you compile, and how much your time is worth, an Athlon 64 upgrade could pay for itself in no time.
Comments like 'UGLY TERRIBLE HACK' tend to indicate good code rather than bad: in bad code ugly terrible hacks are considered par for the course.
In the struggle to meet deadlines, I think pretty much all programmers have put in comments they might later regret, including swearwords and acerbic comments about other code or requirements.
It would therefore be both hypocritical and meaningless to go through the comments looking for embarrassments. But also fun, so let's go.
If we haven't nailed usability for something as simple as a television remote by now – clearly we've got our work cut out for us to make complex software usable.
Buttons proliferate on remotes like rabbits.
There tends to be this conservatism in the design process. I encourage young designers to go off and scare me.
They were designed by – and I hate to say it because I am one of them – engineers.
These are the devilish details that often get overlooked.
I don't think that you reach that level of simple elegance by accident. It's designed the way remotes should be designed.
They work well if you're sitting in bright light and you have good eyesight and you're 20 or 30 years old.
Given the severely limited functionality of Notepad, it's incredible that it has taken me this long to find a suitable replacement for what is, evidently, a core part of my developer toolkit.
The original Notepad shipped with Windows is probably the handiest program of all times, small, fast, without frills!
Look at everything you get for that additional 450kb.
As a developer, it's always helpful to know exactly what assemblies you're loading, and where they are coming from.
As hardware becomes cheaper, software becomes more expensive.
What if software was never free? One answer, obviously: Linux.
Ignorance is bliss, at least, when it comes to someone in Thailand paying less than one-tenth what I am for Office XP and Windows XP.
In a global market, it's inevitable.
It was OK for a retail copy of Windows to cost $99 when computers regularly cost $2,000 to build, circa 1997. That's only five percent of the total cost.
I don't see any reason why, given a strongly tiered per-country pricing structure, the same problems (and pressures) won't come up in software, too.
This is really a no-brainer for any developer who values his or her time.
Nobody answered that they preferred 1 monitor over 2 even a little bit.
All the 'extra stuff' I couldn't fit on the primary or secondary panels finally found a home on the third one.
I think the rule of diminishing returns will definitely kick in for more than three.
I'm also wondering whether I could physically see four monitors without moving my head around a bunch more than I normally do.
I had no idea how significant this feature was until I tried it. It's huge!
I think the ELSEIF keyword is destructive and, like GOTO, has no good use outside of a very specialized niche.
ELSEIF, in my experience, is abused far more than it is used properly.
I have a very hard time coming up with any valid justification for the use of ELSEIF.
98 times out of 100, SELECT CASE is easier to read and offers the CASE ELSE condition, which encourages developers to handle unknown conditions properly.
In the above example, what happens when the dialog returns DialogResult.Cancel?
There's nothing like an extremely tight loop between developers and exceptions to facilitate quick, responsive fixes to broken code.
And since we have it set up to email every team member for every Unhandled Exception – it's a therapeutic form of punishment, too!
I have used the hell out of this code and it works like gangbusters.
This is painful. It means the server becomes unavailable for over three minutes, and any pending requests return errors after ASPNET_WP is cycled.
The best part is, this happens completely randomly. We can't force it to happen or duplicate it, we just have to wait for it to happen.
We went through all the normal troubleshooting procedures and exhausted them all, which left... the tough stuff.

Armed with this information, rather than 'gee, ASPNET_WP is deadlocking a lot,' we were able to determine that the real problem is a pooled connection is not disposed by Microsoft .NET Managed Provider for Oracle when an exception occurs.

The Microsoft System.Data.OracleClient is blindly re-using connections that it knows to be bad, which of generates a NullObjectException, and sooner or later – basically at random – causes ASPNET_WP to fall over and cycle.
Good times... good times...
But as bad as those are, they pale in comparison to what is, hands down, the worst kind of error message: a beautiful, well-formatted, informative, incorrect error message.
Just what you want to do in a production system, make sweeping changes in the back end soon after deployment.
Intellectually, I knew there was no way I could be getting different results from a server call than any other client.
But I still refused to believe this.
I've probably spent more time chasing down erroneous error messages than the time I've spent on all other error messages combined.
Evidently computers, like people, are big fat stinkin' liars!
UNIX will never be usable.
Nobody gives a damn how technically competent your code is when they can't figure out how to use it. Without usability, you have nothing.
Usability is easily an order of magnitude harder than writing technically competent code, even harder than writing your own operating system kernel.
The fact that this mess is an example of our best in action, rather than our worst, just highlights how appallingly low our standards have been.
It is comically unrealistic to ask a community predicated on C code, kernel hacking, and the utility of command line tools, to suddenly wake up and get the usability religion.
Open-source developers don't have time for things that are a pain in the ass – like users, their conflicting needs, and their general disdain for computers and technology.
Good UI design is not a result of black magic, it just requires paying attention.
If I was rich enough to buy a copy of those books for every developer on earth, I would.
There are still far more people who talk about good practices than who actually use good practices.
I see far too many people using current buzzwords as a cloak for sloppy practices.
Most of those people weren't really doing object-oriented programming – they were hacking, and the results were predictable, and poor.
Right now, people are saying 'I don't have to do requirements or design because I'm doing agile development.' Again, the results are easy to predict, and poor.
Good programmers invest the effort to learn how to use current practices. Not-so-good programmers just learn the buzzwords, and that's been a software industry constant for a half century.
These dialogs are frequently abused for displaying all kinds of trivial information to the user, a mistake that Alan Cooper calls stopping the proceedings with idiocy.
This completely breaks any flow of interaction the user had with our app.
Whatever you do, avoid stopping the proceedings with idiocy at all costs.
There's just no question that this is a far better solution than popping a modal dialog with the same information.

It's also better than the 'put an icon in the status bar' solution, because it's more visible, it's at the top of the window where the work starts (nobody sees the status bar), and it contains more information.

The game was constantly sending you notifications of various things going on throughout your dungeon; the notifications would visually flow into a queue with a summary icon to indicate the type and severity of the notification.
What naming scheme do you use for a fully OO language where... everything is an object? objEverything isn't very satisfying.
I do not believe every single object needs a unique prefix. This is insane, and as the VB6 document illustrates, this way leads madness...
Pointing out how stupid someone's variable names are is like telling them how stupid they are for naming their first born child 'Melvin.'
It's more important to be internally consistent with a naming standard than it is to spend a lot of time sussing out some kind of perfect, interplanetary naming standard.
There's nothing quite as soul crushing as over-standardizing in a religious area where there really isn't a 'right' answer.
Naming conventions cannot guarantee good code, however; only the skill of the programmer can.
Every developer should have a good diff tool in their toolkit.
Surprisingly, instead of some crazy proprietary Microsoft format like you'd expect, the file follows the simple Multipart MIME Message RFC standard.
Building an .MHT file is sort of like sending an email to yourself – go figure.
It's workable, but it's kind of... ghetto.
If like me, the only diff tool you ever used was the one in VSS – you may not know how much you're missing.
A start-up's biggest advantage is speed, and speed is what commandos live for.
They work hard, fast, and cheap, though often with a low level of professionalism, which is okay, too, because professionalism is expensive.
They make creativity a destructive act.
While the commandos make success possible, it's the infantry that makes success happen.

There is an enormous difference between starting a company and running one. Thinking up great ideas, which requires mainly intelligence and knowledge, is much easier than building an organization, which also requires measures of tenacity, discipline, and understanding.
Having the wrong group (commandos) at the wrong time (maintenance) can hurt you a lot more than it helps.
These third-wave troops hate change. They aren't troops at all but police.
All Edit and Continue does is tighten the loop between the time a bug is detected, and the time you can fix it. How can this possibly be a bad thing?
Edit and Continue would cut the work I have to do to fix a bug in half. IN HALF!

That is one of the most wrongheaded things I've ever read, and I have to assume it's spoken from ignorance, e.g., developers who have never had this capability in their toolset and therefore don't know what they are missing.
There is a widespread recognition that debugging is the hard and slow part of system programming, and slow turnaround is the bane of debugging.
Harr's data suggest that an interactive facility at least doubles productivity in system programming.

At the time of the exception, you can diagnose the problem – in perfect context of all the live code, which is the easiest way to determine what the fix should be – and make your fix. Then just keep on truckin'.
I generally don't subscribe to the UNIX religion, but there is one area where I am an unabashed convert: regular expressions.
The RegEx is the power drill of the programmer's toolkit: not appropriate for every job, but the go-to tool for a lot of common jobs.
That has always been my complaint about RegEx composition: it's difficult to tell beforehand what the effect of your RegEx will be until you 'run' it and browse all the matches.
Why build what you can steal?
Drunk with the power and possibility of regular expressions, you might start thinking regular expressions can do... well, just about anything.
Despite their undeniable power, RegExps aren't a procedural programming language. In limited string processing roles, they're perfect.
But can you imagine writing an entire application with that kind of crazy, nigh-indecipherable syntax Perl?
For compiling .NET code, the Athlon 64 is 33% faster than a Pentium 4 of the same speed.
Time spent compiling is time wasted staring at the build output window.

Compiling doesn't typically take long enough to force me to ALT+TAB away and start doing something else, so there's no real incentive to multitask... and those wasted seconds add up throughout the day.
You could buy the cheapest Athlon 64 available and still get better .NET compiler performance than the fastest Pentium 4 available.
That's cash in your pocket twice: once for the purchase of the less expensive CPU, and again as you spend less time writing the same code.
Dell has some kind of satanic pact with Intel, and even though the Athlon 64 is a superior technology – in pretty much everything except for media encoding – they will never offer it.
The term 'computer science' is a borderline oxymoron.
Inside a computer, we're back in the dark ages: everyone is God, and the earth is the center of the universe.
Even the term 'software engineering' makes me uneasy; if bridges and airplanes were engineered the same way I 'engineer' software, I'd be afraid to leave the house.
They don't have to build a bridge on Jupiter, or construct an airplane out of brand new, previously unknown materials.
We constantly get the rug pulled out from under us, because there are no rules in our universe – at least, none other than the ones we make up.
Ironically, the only way to achieve anything resembling objective science with computers is to measure things outside the box.
At worst, coding like this screams 'I'm smarter than the Runtime!'
These kinds of developers are, in my experience, dangerous.
Default behaviors are there to make our lives easier, and to protect us – they should be leveraged whenever possible.
Why create extra work for yourself (and the people who will maintain your code) by littering your code with these unnecessary, C++ style Dispose() calls?
At best, this is extra, meaningless code that someone has to debug and read.
I read Coding Slave as the story of a guy from a small business background who was severely traumatized by the world of Large Corporations, Big Six Consulting, and $20 million ERP systems.
One thing I did learn from the book is that the entire concept of an ERP software package is itself kind of, well, insane.

Freely choosing to design a ginormous, monolithic software package like this strikes me as a profoundly bad idea – an idea already tainted by the bizarre mindset of Big Six IT and $20 million ERP packages.
No sane organization, even the completely fictional programmer's guild Bob proposes, would build software this way.

A system 'of the people, and by the people' wouldn't be written at all – it would be absorbed from the various open source projects and patched together communally, feeding back into the larger open source zeitgeist.
Things that are food for litigation in large corporations simply don't exist in small companies.
I'd also argue that the current blogosphere, combined with the open source movement, is a pretty good real world analog for the proposed Ye Olde Programmer's Guild. And it exists today!
I just lost faith that objects are any good on the 'business logic' abstraction level.
Often those fits of cleverness turn into fugues of frustration because the programming techniques designed to reduce complexity have actually compounded it.
At first you're like 'whee! objects!' and then you realize – hey, this is a lot of tedious, error-prone mapping code I didn't have to write before...
Objects lost their place in that picture for me.
The Customer.Create() operation is really no different behaviorally than Product.Create().
This is only useful if it is completely automatic in the IDE, with intellisense support – that is, zero code required from the developer!
I'm probably the last developer on the planet to get wise to the benefits of virtual machine technology.
The additional layer of software translation – emulating an x86 PC on an x86 PC – seemed unnecessary to me, like the VGA port on a flat panel monitor.
It's like having an entire test lab full of machines, loaded with every kind of crazy OS and configuration you can imagine.
A good rule of thumb is that same-system emulation costs about ten percent of raw performance, which is reasonable.
Why not have a powerful server run 5 instances of Windows Server 2000, each blissfully unaware of the other instances?
Evidently the VB code outlining support is completely broken in VS.NET 2003. Why hasn't this gotten more publicity?
I can't believe this hasn't been fixed yet in a patch for VS.NET 2003, because it makes working with #Region – which we use a lot – pretty much impossible.
While it's not a fix, per se, it does add support for #Region outlining at the very least.
If I wanted a single language to bind them all, I'd be writing in Java.
Any of you who feel that the syntax if() { } is somehow morally superior to If... Then End If are fools.
The real choice you make is to use the framework; getting obsessive about the bits of duct tape we use to run the framework through its paces is completely and utterly missing the point.
Language choice is as much an emotional as a logical decision.
In a case where a manager or client insists on paying more for C# development, you have a moral obligation to take their money.
If there's ever a Nobel prize for .NET coding, Lutz is the obvious frontrunner.
Oh, Unix is a world of pain for developers.

The problem with J2EE really is that it became very, very academic and the complexity of all these perfectly designed systems in schools does not necessarily map when you have deadlines and all kinds of other things.
So, it's just because the technology is not as pretty as it could be or as nice as it could be, but it gets the job done.
It certainly helps .NET's credibility a lot in comparison with 'write once, debug everywhere' Java.
It helps developers focus more on what they are doing instead of with the nitty-gritty details of the specific platform.
They could write in C++, but the schedule would just go out the window.
Unless you have a very good reason to catch an exception, DON'T.
Exceptions are supposed to be exceptional, just like the dictionary meaning: uncommon, unusual.
The hardest kinds of exceptions to troubleshoot are the ones that don't even exist, because a developer upstream of you decided to consume it.
When in doubt, let the calling routine, or the global exception handler, deal with it.
Less code is almost always better, IMO.
Always try to catch specific exceptions.
.NET is basically Delphi Reloaded (that's what you get when you hire Anders Hejlsberg away from Borland). It's even better!
The Visual Studio .NET IDE is the gold standard in developer productivity. I'm not aware of any other IDE that even comes close.
Yes, the most usable product is the one people end up using. Go figure.
Where Microsoft goes, so go the Visual Basic developers – the largest group of developers in the world.
Own that mindshare, and you own the world.
The great thing about .NET is that, whatever your opinion of company, Microsoft is gaining mindshare the old fashioned way: by producing a quality product.
In a worst-case scenario, your object's Finalize method won't run until your process terminates gracefully.
Actually, the real worst case is that it never runs at all because the process terminates abnormally.
The Finalize method served no real purpose other than to keep the application from running as fast as it otherwise could.
Just say No to Finalize methods!
In a true worst case scenario – unhandled exception time – you won't get any benefit from Finalize.
It's all rather contradictory.
The only thing that makes sense is that these referenced assemblies are forced to run under the same runtime as the main application, even though they were compiled under 1.0.
Side-by-side testing naturally results in an explosive matrix of test cases, and because of this, there was no explicit testing done for mixing and matching v1.0 and v1.1 frameworks assemblies.
It's disgraceful the way the 1.0 runtime is treated like a third class citizen at Microsoft.
Just try getting a hotfix for a 1.1 bug back-ported into the 1.0 runtime.
We're writing crappy business logic code, not a language.
Inheritance is a specialized tool, and should only be used for situations that truly warrant a parent-child relationship, and all the 'hidden' behavior that entails.
In all of those years, I can think of maybe a half a dozen times, at most, where inheritance was the right choice.
If you really understand inheritance, you may find yourself living the rest of your career without ever creating a single inheritable class or component.
Inheritance is only one way of many to achieve code reuse.
How many of you are writing programming languages? How many of you guys are writing operating system kernels?
To me, the added baggage of inheritance – like all added complexity – is always guilty until proven innocent.
I like the way anyone can leave a comment regarding my posts – the only good discussion is a two-way discussion – but the comment spam is getting way out of hand
SUCK IT SPAMMERS!
The more popular the content is, the more it costs the content owner.
In the world of atoms, mere popularity doesn't cost the content owner a dime.
One puts computing as close to the data as possible in order to avoid expensive network traffic.
Bandwidth is hellaciously expensive relative to almost anything else, other than perhaps labor costs.
We have this weird situation on the internet where popularity is a curse, instead of the blessing it should be.
Unless you are leveraging your popularity to sell something, you are generating a substantial net loss through bandwidth costs.
Highly asymmetric upload/download ratios can be an indirect way for big corporations to enforce copyright restrictions on consumers.
This creates a digital ghetto: 99% of users are locked into the role of ravenous consumers, with massive download capacities but a mere trickle of upload.
I particularly recommend 'Cargo Cult Engineering.'
I spared you guys the embarrassing chicken little-esque Y2K editorial.
I guess we can all take heart, even a guy as smart as Steve gets it wrong sometimes...
What's the point? It's absolutely inevitable that given a sophisticated enough worldwide communications infrastructure, we will begin competing with a people all of the world for the same jobs.
I hate to think of my livelihood reduced to something so disposable that it can be done for a fraction of the cost by cut-rate vendors halfway across the world.
All it takes is ten years of the Internet to bring the Indians to us.
It's OK to make twice as much as an Indian programmer if you're twice as productive.
We dug this hole for ourselves through complacency; now it's time to start digging ourselves out by treating our jobs like an actual engineering profession instead of a license to print money.
Any job that can potentially be done without physically touching something is at risk. And isn't that the very definition of 'knowledge worker?'
Worse-is-better, even in its strawman form, has better survival characteristics than the-right-thing.
x86 is widely regarded as total piece of crap. And it is. But it's a piece of crap honed to an incredibly sharp edge.
Anything with 'Enterprise' in the title, just substitute 'Complicated.'
Whenever possible, always err on the side of simplicity.
Why even write code at all when you can buy or steal open-source it?
Simple solutions survive and prosper because they work, and people can actually understand them.
Don't presume that everyone's smart enough to handle the fancy complex solution; optimism is a dangerous occupational hazard for programmers.
Like words on a page, when you cannot remove any more – you're done.
After three hours of intense frustration, I started to feel like that Doom3 zombie – the one mindlessly pounding his head against a bloody wall over and over.
Eventually you get to that desperate point where you'll try anything, no matter how crazy.
I finally got remote debugging to work with XP SP2 by granting 'Remote Access' to the ANONYMOUS LOGON account in dcomcnfg.exe on the client computer.
That doesn't even make sense; out of the box EVERYONE is granted both local and remote permissions in Edit Limits.
Friends, do not do this.
Two in-place reinstalls later, I had a functioning OS again. Revenge of COM...
A well designed API will win every time, no matter what protocol or interface it is using.
A lot of hand wringing over minor performance differences – tied to what are really implementation details – doesn't buy you much when your bottleneck is elsewhere.
This isn't a COM or CORBA flash in the pan.
It's trivial to switch to binary protocol later; just flick a switch in your .config files.
You should pick the interface that is easiest to troubleshoot and maintain.
Like Notepad, Task Manager is something I use on a daily basis: it's an essential part of my toolkit.
I took a look at some replacements, but I'm so used to Taskman at this point, I can't retrain myself.
And with Task Manager Extension, I don't have to; it adds all the 'missing' features you could possibly want.
Nobody is really smart enough to program computers.
The people who are the worst at programming are the people who refuse to accept the fact that their brains aren't equal to the task.
Your employer can't force you to be a good programmer; a lot of times your employer isn't even in a position to judge whether you're good.
The more you learn to compensate for your small brain, the better a programmer you'll be.
Programming work is essentially unsupervisable because no one ever really knows what you're working on.
Candidates who can't or won't say 'I don't know' get red flagged; those types of programmers are dangerous.
It's not our job to be better than anyone else; we just need to be better than we were a year ago.

One broken window, left unrepaired for any substantial length of time, instills in the inhabitants of the building a sense of abandonment – a sense that the powers that be don't care about the building.
Don't leave 'broken windows' (bad designs, wrong decisions, or poor code) unrepaired. Fix each one as soon as it is discovered.
We've seen clean, functional systems deteriorate pretty quickly once windows start breaking.
Neglect accelerates the rot faster than any other factor.
If you continue to think like that, then you'd better plan on getting a dumpster, or moving to another neighborhood. Don't let entropy win.
It's just so damn... pragmatic.
The mechanical things (coding patterns) are actually quite easy. It's the soft GUI stuff that is difficult, and that's ultimately what makes your product sexy, desirable – and a success.
We may think coding patterns are sexy, but they're invisible to users.
Apple is the only company making a fortune at it, and inspiring fanatical product loyalty. Why? It has the most usable interface.
According to my research, there really is no good way to generically handle unhandled exception in .NET web services.
Put a try...catch around every WebService method. These methods tend to be wrappers around other classes, so this isn't quite as bad as it sounds, but it's still not good.
Write a custom SOAP Extension or HttpModule. This sounds reasonable but... hard. If it's such a cool, important extension or HttpModule, wouldn't someone have written it already?
By default, .NET doesn't throw very good SOAP Exceptions!
You need to re-throw exceptions with some additional data to get the 'optional,' but quite helpful, SOAP <detail> error element populated.
Microsoft is doing their level best to pretend that .NET 1.0 doesn't exist any more
As a developer you're very likely to be running the crippleware version of IIS
Application.DoEvents() - The call of the devil
I had no idea high school seniors were this smart
There is no concept of side by side for assemblies loaded this way. Shocking, but true
I would strongly advise against building object tag 'deployed' .NET apps for anything non-trivial
The success of the project is almost entirely determined by how well these five random builders work together. It has nothing do with skill.
You never know what kind of teammates you're going to get, and you never really know exactly what you're building – until you build it. But by God, it better be done on time!
The major problems of our work are not so much technological as sociological in nature.
By noting the true nature of a problem as sociological rather than political, you make it more tractable.
Failure to take action to deal with a problem employee is the most common complaint that team members have about their leaders.
Teams that communicated well and coordinated their work – with a minimum of friction – invariably succeeded. Teams that argued a lot, and those that broke apart into five individuals... didn't.
Most managers are willing to concede the idea that they've got more people worries than technical worries. But they seldom manage that way.
Some of the worst enemies a developer has are noise or uncertainty in his code, because they prevent him from working with it effectively in the future.
A chunk of code in a disabled state just causes uncertainty.
If the answer to one of these questions is important for people to know, then write a comment spelling it out. Don't leave your co-workers guessing.
I have been angrily accused of deleting someone's commented code on more than one occasion.
Give me a reason not to delete it, and I won't. Otherwise, it's fair game.
In my experience this kind of 'oh, I'll get back to it' code just sits in the codebase forever, junking up the works for every future developer.
I kept getting requests for this code.
I think in the future I'll avoid using my blog to host code of any significance.
formatting the article for that general 'everyprogrammer' audience motivates me to write much more comprehensive documentation than I otherwise would.
I've also gotten some truly excellent feedback/suggestions from developers in the comments.
HTTP compression is the ultimate no-brainer.
The network is really slow, and CPU time is effectively free and getting faster and, uh, "free-er" every day.
Every website should be serving up HTTP compressed pages to clients that can accept it.
Despite what the MS documentation says, the metabase filename extension lists are not space delimited! They are cr/lf delimited.
It's truly amazing how much faster pages appear to load, even over a 100baseT local network, with HTTP compression enabled.
Nobody is writing new operating systems any more. The world has devolved into UNIX and NT camps exclusively.
Unix is like Cutler's lifelong foe. It's like his Moriarty. He thinks Unix is a junk operating system designed by a committee of Ph.D.s.
Microsoft didn't really manage much at all: they just chose to build the company with the smartest people they could find and let them figure it out.
If you can't explain what you plan to do in practical, meaningful terms – you're probably full of crap.
Ten years from now we'll be designing another system, and everyone will be sitting around bemoaning that it will have to be compatible with NT. That will happen.
I think it's possible we may not see another 'from the ground up' OS developed in our lifetimes.
Always measure performance. Never assume anything will be faster or slower until you've actually measured it to be so – you'll be surprised how often your assumptions are wrong.
Sometimes the way you measure performance can even be flawed.
Rules of Optimization: Rule 1: Don't do it. Rule 2 (for experts only): Don't do it yet.
Don't optimize work that doesn't have to be done.
0.1 second is about the limit for having the user feel that the system is reacting instantaneously.
10 seconds is about the limit for keeping the user's attention focused on the dialogue.
Why not build all of our links using relative Google search terms?
Now you have a permanent, unbreakable link to that content.
Google would become unbelievably powerful. They would be the 'link DNS' for the entire internet.

Whenever I link to articles in my code, I try to do so through very specific google search terms, which are likely to produce valid links many years from now – even if the content moves to a different place on the internet.
With the rise of REST, XML, SOAP and .NET Remoting in the last year, sniffing has become an essential part of my development toolkit.
Etherdetect isn't free, and it isn't perfect, but it offers the best blend of functionality and ease of use that I've found.
Peeking behind the scenes at network traffic has solved some tough performance and debugging problems in our .NET apps.
You typically can't sniff traffic going to localhost, at least not without some special workarounds; the loopback TCP/IP stack behaves very differently than the normal network paths.
The Net.WebClient class doesn't support HTTP compression
And voila, the bandwidth, you have saved eet!
First of all, them's fightin' words, and second, those results are just too crazy to really make sense.
Why would C# be over an order of magnitude slower than Java at a classic computer science problem like a regular expression parser? I don't believe it.
So... yeah. I don't know what kind of crack the guys at manageability.org are smoking, but I can't seem to find a local vendor.

Unlike virtually every other Windows OS applet of similar vintage I still use it every day. That's a testament to how well Task Manager was originally designed.
Sorting column headers is probably the single most powerful function in task manager. You can diagnose almost any performance problem by sorting the right process column!
Commit charge is the single most important section of the performance tab.
If the peak commit charge is greater than the physical memory in your PC, your PC is running out of memory and thrashing.
You want a reasonable amount of free memory, but free memory is also wasted memory – it should be utilized as disk cache whenever possible.

Developers present estimates that are optimistic. Executives like the optimistic estimates because they imply that desirable business targets are achievable.

Imagine a computer that is the size of a grain of sand that can test keys against some encrypted data. Also imagine that it can test a key in the amount of time it takes light to cross it. Then consider a cluster of these computers, so many that if you covered the earth with them, they would cover the whole planet to the height of 1 meter. The cluster of computers would crack a 128-bit key on average in 1,000 years.
And of course, there are always 256-bit keys, if you worry about the possibility that government has a spare planet that they want to devote to key-cracking.
It is very easy to underestimate the power of exponentials.
If we want to double the amount of time the brute force attack will take, all we need to do is tack on one teeny, tiny little bit to our key.
It's painfully clear that a brute force attack on even a 128 bit key is a fool's errand. Even if you're using a planet covered with computers that crack keys at the speed of light.
If you're a smart attacker, you already know that brute force key attacks are strictly for dummies with no grasp of math or time.

Since SQL joins appear to be set-based, the use of Venn diagrams to explain them seems, at first blush, to be a natural fit.
Inner join produces only the set of records that match in both Table A and Table B.
Full outer join produces the set of all records in Table A and Table B, with matching records from both sides where available.
To produce the set of records only in Table A, but not in Table B, we perform the same left outer join, then exclude the records we don't want from the right side via a where clause.
This joins 'everything to everything,' resulting in 4 x 4 = 16 rows, far more than we had in the original sets.
If you do the math, you can see why this is a very dangerous join to run against large tables.

These two modes aren't mere labels; they're enforced by the CPU hardware.
Instead of your entire system crashing, only that particular application crashes. That's the value of User mode.
Do these drivers run in Kernel mode, for maximum performance, or do they run in User mode, for maximum stability?
Transitioning between User and Kernel mode is expensive. Really expensive.
User mode is clearly a net public good, but it comes at a cost.

The CPU's strict segregation of code between User and Kernel mode is completely transparent to most of us, but it is quite literally the difference between a computer that crashes all the time and a computer that crashes catastrophically all the time.

What's amazing to me about Markov chains is how unbelievably simple they are.

A Markov chain has no memory of previous states: the next state is chosen based on a random dice roll and a lookup into a table of the states that tend to historically follow the current state in the input corpus.
Given an adequate input corpus, they work almost uncannily well, a testament to the broad power of rudimentary statistical inference.
PageRank can be understood as a Markov chain in which the states are pages, and the transitions are all equally probable and are the links between pages.
But Markov chains aren't just useful for automatically generating Paul Graham essay parodies. They're also quite practical.
If you play with the Markov text synthesizer, you'll quickly find that Markov methods are only as good as their input corpus.
But it's sure tough to imagine a more ideal input corpus for Markovian techniques than the unimaginable vastness of web and email, isn't it?

There's simply no reason for MSTest to exist except to satisfy some bizarre corporate directive that Microsoft never ship open source code in their products.
It's a clone of NUnit with all new bugs and no new features, but it can be included in the box with Visual Studio and integrated into the product.
The more I think about this, the more I think Microsoft's utter inability to integrate open source software of any kind whatsoever into their products might just end up killing them.
The best way to 'beat' open source is to join 'em – to integrate with and ship open source components as a part of your product.
Source control is the bedrock of modern software engineering, and I've found precious few developers that truly understand how it works.
If I worked in the upper echelons of Microsoft, I'd be looking at the graph of open source software growth from the years of 1999 to 2008 and crapping my pants right about now.
You can't expect to get a decent chair for less than $500.
A quality chair is so comfortable and accommodating it effortlessly melts into the background, so you can focus on your work.
A cheesy, cheap chair constantly reminds you how many hours of work you have left.
I can't think of any other piece of equipment I use in my job that has lasted me ten full years and beyond.
Choice of seating is as fundamental and constant as it gets in a programming career otherwise marked by relentless change.
Why not take the same care and consideration in selecting a chair as you would with the other strategic directions that you'll carry with you for the rest of your career?
By my calculations, the Aeron cost me about $7 per month over its ten year lifetime; I figure my continued health and comfort while programming are worth at least that much.
I can't see anything! I have to manually expand those sections to browse any of the code in this class.
Code that hides from you is code that will rot in the most putrescent and painful way possible.
I daresay being able to see the damn code is more important than having it meticulously segmented into six pointless little named buckets.
Your code should be front and center at all times – exposed to as many programmers' eyes, and as much healing light, as possible.
If the code needs the crutch of folding to look organized, it's bad code.
Why, exactly, are we writing code to accommodate the editor?
I've always been wary of cowboy coders who rolled into an ongoing project on fresh horses and immediately started dictating terms.
As I grow older and wiser even older as a programmer, I've found that my personal coding style has trended heavily toward minimalism.
I've tried to code smaller, even going so far as to write no code at all when I can get away with it.
Minimize variability of variables. Strive to make variables final in Java and const in C++.
Define variables at the smallest possible scope.
Minimize lifetime of variables. Prefer ephemeral variables to longer lived ones.
Minimalism isn't always the right choice, but it's rarely the wrong choice.
You could certainly do worse than to adopt the discipline of spartan programming on your next programming project.
Is there anything more anti-web than demanding users install custom software to display information that could have just as easily been delivered through the browser?
Or, you could bludgeon me with the digital equivalent of a giant stop sign.
Hey, 'it just works.' Except when it doesn't.
Let's imagine, for a moment, how utterly ridiculous it would be if I had to install the Amazon application to browse and purchase media from Amazon.
It's downright user hostile to demand installation of a special application merely to browse the store, and it is most certainly against everything the web stands for and was built on.
The last 'application' I can recall needing to install to get to things online was AOL.
Try to imagine a world where every programmer you know is a wannabe language designer, bent on molding the language to their whims.
When I close my eyes and imagine it, I have a vision of the apocalypse, a perfect, pitch-black storm of utterly incomprehensible, pathologically difficult to debug code.
I know enough about programmers – myself absolutely included – to know the vast majority of us have absolutely no business whatsoever re-designing a programming language.
Is there anything more pure and godlike than programming your own programming language?
But if wielding that power doesn't scare and humble you a little, too, then maybe you should leave the monkeypatching to the really smart monkeys.

Patches interact in unpredictable, combinatoric ways. And by their nature, bugs caused by monkey patches are more difficult to track down than those introduced by more traditional classes and methods.
Monkeypatching should mean reaching for the meta-hammer as infrequently as humanly possible.
Everything is fast for small n.
Which is better—a normalized database, or a denormalized database? Trick question! The answer is that it doesn't matter!
Scalability is not your problem, getting people to give a shit is.
Never, never should you normalize a database out of some vague sense of duty to the ghosts of Boyce-Codd.
Normalization is not magical fairy dust you sprinkle over your database to cure all ills; it often creates as many problems as it solves.
Fear not the specter of denormalization.
Normalize until it hurts, denormalize until it works.
Some developers love to gold plate their software.
It's usually considered wasteful to fritter away time gold plating old code in the face of new features that need to be implemented, or old bugs that could be squashed.
Jeff wrote in support for an analog rotary controller... that did not exist.
That's the very definition of 'not required.'
This code lied dormant in the game until a handful of enthusiasts, fourteen years later, cobbled together custom controllers to play the game as it was originally intended by the author.
If that isn't the ultimate case of gold plating your software, I don't know what is.
At the point where you're actively campaigning against the project, and working against your teammates – you're a liability to the project.
You don't have to be friends with everyone on your team, although it certainly helps, but a level of basic personal and professional respect is mandatory for any team to function normally.
More than any other single aspect of team leadership, members are disturbed by leaders who are unwilling to deal directly and effectively with self-serving or noncontributing team members.
You can develop skill, but you can't develop a positive attitude.
If your team leader or manager isn't dealing with the bad apples on your project, she isn't doing her job.
Removing someone from a team is painful; it's not fun for anyone. But realizing you should have removed someone six months ago is far more painful.
They'll slowly spread poison throughout your project, in the form of code, relationships, and contacts.
If you're not careful bordering on manically fastidious in constructing your Views, you'll end up with a giant mish-mash of HTML, Javascript, and server-side code.
Now you can double your pleasure: badly formed HTML, meet badly written code.
Do you find this readable? Can you see where the code begins and the markup ends?

Sometimes editing this stuff makes me feel like I'm playing Operation. I have to ever so carefully maneuver my metal tweezers into one tiny slice of code or HTML and make my changes without touching the edges and setting off that blasted electrical buzzer.
The tag soup problem seems to be endemic to all modern web development stacks.
Perhaps when it comes to mixing HTML and server-side code, some form of soup is unavoidable, a necessary evil.
Or is this year's newer, fancier, even-more-delicious iteration of tag soup as good as it ever gets for web development?
This thing is, for all intents and purposes, a complete, standalone x86 PC that fits in the palm of your hand and sips five watts of power.
That's almost ten times less than my HTPC build I was so proud of!
You may recall those now-ancient boxes were still able to do some pretty amazing things in their day.

I would not build an ultra-lower power PC assuming it will be tolerable for day-to-day web browsing and email reading, unless you're comfortable using text mode or command-line interfaces exclusively.
With the emergence of Intel's Atom and 'netbooks' like the ASUS Eee, it's a segment that is dangerously close to becoming mainstream.
What could you do with a tiny, highly efficient x86 PC that boots up in under a minute?
You should always write your code as if comments didn't exist.
The code already tells us how it works; we need the comments to tell us why it works.
Junior developers rely on comments to tell the story when they should be relying on the code to tell the story.
To write good comments you have to be a good writer.
If your feel your code is too complex to understand without comments, your code is probably just bad.
Comments are narrative asides; important in their own way, but in no way meant to replace plot, characterization, and setting.

I think maybe some of us might be better off sticking to our strengths – that is, writing for the compiler, in as clear a way as we possibly can, and reaching for the comments only as a method of last resort.
Maybe this is a minority opinion, but I find understanding the hardware to be instructive for programmers.
If you can snap together a Lego kit, you can build a computer.
Despite what Intel's marketing weasels might want you to believe, clock speed still matters very much.
Outside of a few niche activities, such as video encoding and professional 3D rendering, most computing tasks don't scale worth a damn beyond two cores.
By the time your system has to reach outside of the L1, L2, and possibly even L3 cache – it's already so slow from the system's perspective as to be academic.
It's all about putting the money where the most significant bottlenecks are.
An efficient power supply will run cooler and more reliably because it uses higher quality parts.
One of the hidden value propositions in building your own PC is the ability to easily upgrade it later.
Open source projects run on time, not money.
I was crushingly disappointed to find out the $5,000 in grant money has been sitting in the bank for the last four months, totally unused.
Open Source is to Traditional Software as Terror Cells are to Large Standing Armies – if you gave a terrorist group a fighter jet, they wouldn't know what to do with it.
Why did we bother doing this if nothing changes?
I'm absolutely dumbfounded to learn that contributing money isn't an effective way to advance an open source project.
That's painful to hear, possibly the most painful of all outcomes.
The term beta test comes from an IBM hardware product test convention, dating back to punched card tabulating and sorting machines.
One of the most experienced software developers I ever worked with characterized the release candidate development phase thusly: 'does this bug kill small children?'
The software is finished – and by finished, we mean there are no show-stopping, little-children-killing bugs in it. That we know of.
The definition of beta grows more all-encompassing and elastic every year.
We are awfully eager to throw alpha quality code over the wall to external users and testers.
In the brave new world of web 2.0, the alpha and beta designations don't mean quite the same things they used to.
'loves you' is certainly more fun than 'gold,' but I'm not sure it's ever the same as done.
The works of highest quality were all produced by the group being graded for quantity.

While the 'quantity' group was busily churning out piles of work – and learning from their mistakes – the 'quality' group had sat theorizing about perfection, and in the end had little more to show for their efforts than grandiose theories and a pile of dead clay.
Quantity always trumps quality.
Until you've mentally committed to doing it over and over, you will not improve. You can't.
If you aren't building, you aren't learning.
Rather than agonizing over whether you're building the right thing, just build it.
Most software project management books are crap. Just most of them.

This software developer does not have a detailed list of all the things he needs to do. Which means, despite adamantly claiming that he is 99 percent done – he has no idea how long development will take!
Until you've got at least the beginnings of a task list, any concept of scheduling is utter fantasy.
I'm almost pathologically bad about writing things down. Unless I'm writing a blog entry, I suppose.
Every time someone asks you what your schedule is, you should be able to point to a list of everything you need to do.
If you can't – the first item on your task list should be to create that list.
In short: we broke people. It was like a Vietnamese prison camp in stereo.
Maybe it's so bad, it has actually wrapped all the way around and it's... good again?
It was hard to tell which was more loathesome – Nabors gone gospel or Cutler gone musical.
Music licensing is the hardest problem in computer science!
So the next time you ask one of your fellow programmers to put on some background coding music for the team, think twice.
JavaScript now works. Just look around you on the web.
It is excruciatingly painful to write and test your complex JavaScript code across (n) browsers and (n) operating systems. It'll make you pine for the good old days of HTML 4.0 and CGI.
The JavaScript Ninjas have delivered their secret and ultimate weapon: common APIs.
They transform working with JavaScript from an unpleasant, write-once-debug-everywhere chore into something that's actually – dare I say it – fun.
Frankly, it is foolish to even consider rolling your own JavaScript code to do even the most trivial of things in a browser now.
Using JQuery while writing Stack Overflow is probably the only time in my entire career as a programmer that I have enjoyed writing JavaScript code.
It's sure pleasant to write code against solid, increasingly standardized JavaScript API libraries that spackle over all those infuriating browser differences.
If it can happen to us, it can happen to anyone. Which means FUI can be quite dangerous in the wrong hands.
You and I may understand that distinction, but what about your parents? Your wife? Your children? Your less technically savvy friends?
It's increasingly difficult to tell the good guys from the bad guys. That's the downside of fear as a selling point: it cuts equally well in both directions.

Can you imagine your mother doing a web search on flowers – flowers, for God's sake – clicking on the search results to a totally legitimate website, and correctly navigating the resulting maze of fake UI, spurious javascript alerts, and download dialogs?
If the spoofing is good enough, the FUI extra-convincing, even a Linux or OS X user could be coerced into entering their admin password for a 'system security scan.'
Short of user education, which is a never ending, continuous uphill battle – how would you combat a perfectly spoofed FUI presented to a naïve user?
Check in early, check in often.
If the code isn't checked into source control, it doesn't exist.
I'd much rather have small fragments checked in periodically than to go long periods with no idea whatsoever what my coworkers are writing.
I'm not proposing developers check in broken code – but I also argue that there's a big difference between broken code and incomplete code.

Developers that wouldn't even consider adopting the old-school waterfall method of software development somehow have no problem adopting essentially the very same model when it comes to their source control habits.
Start with a tiny fragment of code that does almost nothing. Look on the bright side – code that does nothing can't have many bugs!
You always have functional software. It may not do much, but it runs.

It's as if one of the dining philosophers happened to glance over at another philosopher's plate, and the other philosopher, seeing this, screamed 'meal viewing deadlock!' and quickly covered his plate with his hands.
In theory there is no difference between theory and practice. In practice there is.
You'll find no shortage of database architecture astronauts who start dropping ACID science on you and all but pull the building fire alarm when you tell them you want to try nolock.
I'm deeply uncomfortable with errors I don't fully understand. This is the kind of stuff that quite literally keeps me up at night.
With nolock / read uncommitted / dirty reads, data may be out of date at the time you read it, but it's never wrong or garbled or corrupted in a way that will crash you.
I'm a little disappointed that SQL Server treats our silly little web app like it's a banking application.
It ain't rocket surgery.
XSS is now the most common of all publicly reported security vulnerabilities – dwarfing old standards like buffer overruns and SQL injection.
Once someone has your browser cookies for a given website, they essentially have the keys to the kingdom for your identity there.
It's sort of ironic that the HttpOnly flag was pioneered by Microsoft in hoary old Internet Explorer 6 SP1, a browser which isn't exactly known for its iron-clad security record.
HttpOnly cookies don't make you immune from XSS cookie theft, but they raise the bar considerably.
It's practically free, a 'set it and forget it' setting that's bound to become increasingly secure over time.
If you develop web applications, or you know anyone who develops web applications, make sure they know about HttpOnly cookies.
I debated for days which geeky reference I would use as a synonym for 'we're having a baby.' The title is the best I could do. I'm truly sorry.
Despite the best of intentions, you really start to resent all those teenage couples who manage to get pregnant so awkwardly and accidentally.
It doesn't take special talents to reproduce – even plants can do it. On the other hand, contributing to a program like Emacs takes real skill.
I'm looking forward to stocking our unborn child's mind with all my insane, crazy ideas.
We are dangerous. We are daring and immortal.
I guess what I'm trying to say is that, with any luck, he or she will be scarred for life. That's a proud family tradition where I come from.
Stack Overflow is sort of like the anti-experts-exchange (minus the nausea-inducing sleaze and quasi-legal search engine gaming) meets wikipedia meets programming reddit.
There's far too much great programming information trapped in forums, buried in online help, or hidden away in books that nobody buys any more.
We are all, as developers, hopelessly incompetent.

We all have these little tiny pieces of information and if we can just contribute a little bit, that information gets amplified, and maybe a thousand other dumb developers will benefit from my one little piece of good information.
The idea that you have all these experts waiting in the wings to do stuff is an illusion in my experience. There's really just a bunch of amateurs muddling along trying to do things together.
The people that are truly experts are too busy to even help, right? And if the experts are too busy to help, what difference does it really make if there are experts at all.
But who cares what I think; my opinion holds no particular weight. I'm just a member. This is our site.
The goal of advertising is to get people talking about it. Even if every one of those conversations starts with 'what the hell were they thinking,' hey – it's a conversation.
I'd much sooner associate myself with Hodgman than with Long.
The sleight of hand breaks down a bit when you realize that Hodgman actually uses Macs, but that's advertising for you: a giant pack of lies.
The fact that a design uses inheritance and polymorphism doesn't make it a good design.
On the whole, I'd call these ads opaque bordering on inane.
Since XSS gets all the limelight, few developers pay much attention to another form of attack that's equally destructive and potentially far easier to exploit.
Your application can be vulnerable to cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks not because you the developer did something wrong, but simply because of how the whole Web is designed to work. Scary!
From the webserver's perspective, there is no difference whatsoever between a real user initiated browser request and the above image URL retrieval.
With XSRF, malicious users can initiate any arbitrary action they like on a target website.
I thought we had our website vulnerabilities licked with XSS. I was wrong.
Don't make the same mistake I did! Understand how XSRF works and ensure you're protected before it becomes a problem.
When we say 'web search' we mean one thing, and one thing only: Google.
Google's current position as the start page for the internet kind of scares the crap out of me, in a way that Microsoft's dominance over the desktop PC never did.
Do we really trust one company to be a benevolent monopoly over... well, everything?

Writing search code is hard. It's far easier to outsource the burden of search to Google and their legions of server farms than it is for our tiny development team to do it on our one itty-bitty server.
Sometimes I'd even get copies of our content from evil RSS scraper sites that plug in their own ads of questionable provenance, which was downright depressing.
I'm a little aggravated that we have to set up this special file for the Googlebot to do its job properly.
But when 50% of your traffic comes from one source, perhaps it's best not to ask these kinds of questions.
I, for one, welcome our pixelated Google overlords!

The key to understanding CSRF attacks is to recognize that websites typically don't verify that a request came from an authorized user. Instead they verify only that the request came from the browser of an authorized user.
Attackers are gambling that users have a validated login cookie for your website already stored in their browser.
If major public facing websites are falling prey to these serious XSRF exploits, how confident do you feel that you haven't made the same mistakes?
I'm saying this as a developer who has already made these same mistakes on his own website. I'm just as guilty as anyone.
It's our job to make sure future developers don't repeat the same stupid mistakes we made – at least not without a fight.
If you want to audit a website for XSRF vulnerabilities, start by asking this simple question about every single HTML form you can find: 'where's the XSRF value?'
Being a 'professional' developer, if there really is such a thing – I still have my doubts – doesn't mean choosing third-party libraries for every possible programming task you encounter.
If it's a core business function – do it yourself, no matter what.
What I cannot create, I do not understand.
I'm not so much talented as competent and loud.
In choosing to fight that HTML sanitizer battle, I've earned the scars of experience.
I don't have to take anybody's word for it – I don't have to trust 'libraries.'
Surely there's something, some core business competency, so important that you feel compelled to build it yourself, even if it means making your own mistakes.
Every day a brand new software developer is born and immediately begins repeating all the same mistakes we made years ago.
Obscenity filters are like blacklists; using one is tantamount to admitting failure before you've even started.
I'm doubtful it will ever be possible to solve this particular problem through code alone.
Oh, great, an inexperienced developer had a problem, and thought they would use regular expressions. Now they have two problems.
Replacing one thing with another, when it comes to human beings and written language, is an activity that is fraught with peril even in the best possible circumstances.
Bad ideas are just plain bad ideas, regardless of language or implementation choices.
If there was one thing I could teach every engineer, it would be how to market.
All that brilliant code is totally irrelevant until people understand what you're doing, become interested in what you're doing, and get excited about what you're doing.
At its core, marketing is little more than an intermediate level course on fundamental human communication.
You probably spent too much time building your code and not enough time as an advocate of your code.
Sometimes, you become a better programmer by choosing not to program.
OK, you're a genius programmer who can code circles around everyone else. But you may never ship any of your code for reasons that you don't control. That's an illusion.
I had been reading dirty books. Marketing books. By choice, even.
When you say that the information presented on a blog is 'dangerous,' you're implying the audience is too dumb or inept to read critically.
I am a better programmer for having read that blog post. Even though, near as I can tell, it's offering inaccurate advice.
The best content always begins where the blog post ends.
Like any veteran internet user, I never take what I read on a blog – or any other site on the internet, for that matter – as fact. Rather, it's a germ of an intriguing idea, a call to action.
Maybe what I promote on this blog is dangerous: thinking for yourself.
My audience is far, far smarter than I will ever be.
I, for one, have too much respect for my audience to ever do that.
At this point, who cares what operating system you run?
Choice of web browser will have a far more profound impact on most people's daily lives.
Netbooks are an entirely different breed of animal. They are cheap, portable web browsers.
The most popular application in the world is the web browser. By far. Number two isn't even close.
Pitching the web browser as a bona-fide operating system always seemed stupid to me. Or at least it did, until I sat down with my first netbook.
This is a huge opening for Linux; it's the ideal way to deliver a complete, modern web browser at nearly zero marginal cost to both the vendor and consumer.
If it runs Firefox 3, or Chrome, or Opera, that's about all I need.
URLs are simple things. Or so you'd think.
What's truly aggravating is that parens in URLs are perfectly legal.
You could force users to escape the parens, but that's sort of draconian, and it's a little unreasonable to expect your users to know how to escape characters in the URL.
Allowing arbitrary random characters in URLs is setting yourself up for XSS exploits, and I can tell you that from personal experience.
That's a whole lot of extra work, just because the URL spec allows parens.
Avoid using parens (or any unusual characters, for that matter) in URLs you create. They're annoying to use, and rarely handled correctly by auto-linking code.
All those trappings of modern computing that we take for granted today? Engelbart demonstrated them all two years before I was born.
Engelbart was so far ahead of his time in 1968 that his demonstration wasn't taken seriously – it seemed absurd and impractical.
It really makes you wonder which of today's HCI researchers we're ignoring but shouldn't be.
Reading this reminds me how far we've come, and yet how far we have to go.
It only took, what, thirty-six years?
I cringe every time I use a browser for many reasons.
The browser people had a chance to make a more integrated UI and functionality, but really did pretty much the opposite in almost all respects.
Intel did a fantastic job on the Atom CPU, but they completely phoned it in on the chipset.
I think netbooks will be as revolutionary as Kay originally predicted with his DynaBook concept.
Only 1 or 2 watts of the total ~25 watt idle power draw is attributable to the CPU, a tiny fraction of the overall power consumption.

Writing programs that the computer can understand is challenging, to be sure. That's why so few people, in the big scheme of things, become competent programmers. But writing paragraphs and sentences that your fellow humans can understand – well, that's even more difficult.
You have to write programs that can be understood by both the computer and your fellow programmers.

Of all the cruel tricks in software engineering, this has to be the cruelest. Most of us entered this field because the machines are so much more logical than people. And yet, even when you're writing code explicitly intended for the machine, you're still writing. For other people.
The practitioner of literate programming can be regarded as an essayist, whose main concern is with exposition and excellence of style.

Vigorous writing is concise. A sentence should contain no unnecessary words, a paragraph no unnecessary sentences, for the same reason that a drawing should have no unnecessary lines and a machine no unnecessary parts.
Most of us spend our entire lives learning how to write effectively.
Coding, after all, is just writing. How hard can it be?
Hello, my name is Jeff Atwood, and I'm an addict. I'm addicted... to video cards.
So if you want to brag, you can say your graphics card has approximately the same compute power as 40 P5-200 chips.
There's nothing quite like having a little chat on usenet with the founder of the company who created the 3D accelerator you just bought. Like I said, it was a simpler time.
Every year since then I've made a regular pilgrimage to the temple of the GPU Gods, paying my tithe and bringing home whatever the latest, greatest, state-of-the art in 3D accelerators happens to be.
Pity the poor rubes who bought these cards at launch! Hey, wait a second – I've been one of those rubes for 10 years now.
That's what separates us from those knuckle-dragging Mac users: skill.
I do love this new video card, but I say that every year. I try not to grow too attached. I'm sure this video card will be replaced in a year with something even better.
Stop words are words so common they are typically ignored for search purposes.
'The' is one of the most common words in the English language, so a naïve search for 'The The' rarely ends well.
You'd think a pure count of frequency, how often the word occurs, would be enough to make a common group of words 'stop words,' but apparently not everyone agrees.
I ended up with a MySQL database of 110,000 English words and their frequency in Google as of late summer 2004.
Indeed, I wonder if classic search stop words are relevant in modern computing; perhaps they're a relic of early 90's computing that we haven't quite left behind yet.
Sometimes words and phrases that might be considered stopwords or stop-phrases may actually be meaningful or important.
Apparently, at least to Google, stop word warnings are a thing of the past.
NP-complete problems are like hardcore pornography. Nobody can define what makes a problem NP-complete, exactly, but you'll know it when you see it.
What do expert programmers do when faced by an intractable problem? They cheat. And so should you!
Sometimes coming up with clever cheats can be more interesting than searching in vain for the perfect solution.
I can't find an efficient algorithm, but neither can all these famous people.
The time required to solve even moderately large versions of many of these problems easily reaches into the billions or trillions of years, using any amount of computing power available today.

An expert programmer should be able to recognize an NP-complete problem so that he or she does not unknowingly waste time trying to solve a problem which so far has eluded generations of computer scientists.
Like broken clocks, even pointy-haired managers are right once a day. Coding is just typing.
We are typists first, and programmers second.
I can't understand why professional programmers out there allow themselves to have a career without teaching themselves to type. It doesn't make any sense.
When you're a fast, efficient typist, you spend less time between thinking that thought and expressing it in code.
I have a friend, John, who can only use one of his hands. He types 70 wpm. He invented his own technique for it. He's not making excuses; he's typing circles around people who are making excuses.
Don't just type random gibberish as fast as you can on the screen, unless you're a Perl programmer.
There's precious little a programmer can do without touching the keyboard; it is the primary tool of our trade.
There's no difference between a bug and a feature request from the user's perspective.
I think most bug tracking systems fail us because they make us ask the wrong questions.
Party like it's 1996, folks, because you'll get MS Sans Serif, and you'll like it.
Nudge things you don't want to do into that 'feature request' bucket, and proceed to ignore them forever.
Argue strongly and loudly enough that something reported as a 'bug' clearly isn't, and you may not have to do any work to fix it.
Stop dividing the world into Bugs and Feature Requests, and both of these project pathologies go away.

I wish we could, as an industry, spend less time fighting tooth and nail over definitions, painstakingly placing feedback in the 'bug' or 'feature request' buckets – and more time doing something constructive with our users' feedback.
As professional programmers, I think we're all naturally inclined to see these sort of low-bid contract sites as cannibalizing and cheapening our craft.

You'll be amazed at how little people in some parts of the world will bid. That's because a few dollars an hour can work well in a country where the average wage is a couple of hundred dollars a month.
What ends up happening in these cases is that usually somebody ends up getting ripped off (either the client or the consultant who underbid or fails to deliver).
Specification documents are hard enough to write when everyone involved is a coworker sitting in the same room.
They might be one way to find a client relationship, but I'd be highly suspicious of any professional developer who can't find a stable, long term relationship with a client eventually.
Surely you can demonstrate better value to your employer than the random, anonymous programmers on Elance, guru.com, RentACoder, or oDesk.

Email is such a funny thing. People hand you these single little messages that are no heavier than a river pebble. But it doesn't take long until you have acquired a pile of pebbles that's taller than you and heavier than you could ever hope to move.
The underlying problem is that individual human beings don't scale.
The net number of requests for my attention exceeds my ability to provide that attention by at least an order of magnitude.
Why answer one person's email directly when I could potentially answer a thousand different people's email with a single blog post?
We should avoid sending email out of a deep respect for our peers – so that they are free to communicate as effectively and as often as possible with us.
I've been desperately clawing my way out from under this mountain of pebbles.
I think we're leaving one metaphor on the table which more accurately reflects the way software is built in the real world: flail around randomly and pray you succeed by force of pure dumb luck.
Software is soft; buildings aren't.
All the best software projects I've worked were, for lack of a better word, alive.

The computer performance shell game, also known as 'find the bottleneck,' is always played between these four resources: CPU, Disk, Network, Memory.
At any given moment, your computer is waiting for some operation to complete on one of these resources.
If you're interested in performance, the absolute first thing you have to do is determine which of these bottlenecks is currently impeding performance – and eliminate it.
At which point the bottleneck often shifts to some other part of the system, far too rapidly for your eye to see.

We're so ecstatic to get that single useful email out of hundreds that we can't keep ourselves from compulsively pressing the new email lever over and over and over, hoping it will happen again soon, like the caged rats in Skinners' experiments.
When we treat email as the kitchen sink of communication, appropriate for everything, it simply ceases to work at all.

Netbooks are the endpoint of four decades of computing – the final, ubiquitous manifestation of 'A PC on every desk and in every home.'

Have you ever opened a simple little ASCII text file to see it inexplicably displayed as onegiantunbrokenline?
Did you ever wonder what was at the end of your lines? As a programmer, I knew there were end of line characters, but I honestly never thought much about them. They just... worked.

Confusing terms based on archaic hardware that is no longer in use, and is confounding to new users who have no point of reference for said terms; Completely arbitrary platform 'standards' for what is exactly the same function. Pretty much business as usual in computing.
The separation of the two functions concealed the fact that the print head could not return from the far right to the beginning of the next line in one-character time.
Even worse, it's possible to mix all three of these line endings in the same file.
Innocently copy and paste a comment or code snippet from a file with a different set of line endings, then save it. Bam, you've got a file with multiple line endings. That you can't see.
I find it fascinating that the mundane ASCII newline has so much ancient computing lore behind it, and that it still regularly bites us in unexpected places.
The Great Newline Schism is something you need to be aware of.

Querying the database is so slow (relative to typical code execution) that if you have to ask how long it will take, you can't afford it.
Stack Overflow has dozens to hundreds of plain vanilla uncompiled Linq to SQL queries on every page. What we don't have is 'scalability nightmares.'
Each uncompiled run of the query took less than one third of a millisecond longer.

At first I was worried that every Stack Overflow page was 1.5 times slower than it should be. But then I realized it's probably more realistic to make sure that any page we generate isn't doing 500 freakin' thousand queries!

Password rules are bullshit.
These days, given the state of cloud computing and GPU password hash cracking, any password of 8 characters or less is perilously close to no password at all.
They heavily penalize your ideal audience, people that use real random password generators.
They frustrate average users, who then become uncooperative and use 'creative' workarounds that make their passwords less secure.
If you don't allow (almost) every single Unicode character in the password input field, you are probably doing it wrong.

There's no question that a hacker will submit these common passwords in a hack attempt – and it's shocking how far you can get, even with aggressive password attempt rate limiting, using just the 1,000 most common passwords.
These aren't hypothetical synthetic Jack the Ripper password rules some bored programmer dreamed up, these are real passwords used by real users.
In short, try to think outside the password input box, like a user would.
